title: [lichen_util.h:73-77] secure_zero word-write only reads back first byte
description:
Gap found: secure_zero verification not complete.

Why this was missed: Algorithm analyzed but read-back pattern not verified.

The actual bug: Lines 73-77 write a zero word using memcpy, then read back via volatile:
  __builtin_memcpy((void *)p, &zero_word, sizeof(unsigned long));
  (void)*(volatile uint8_t *)p;  // Read-back
  
The read-back only reads 1 byte (uint8_t), not the full word. On a platform where:
1. memcpy might be optimized away
2. The compiler observes only 1 byte is read

A sophisticated optimizer could theoretically recognize that only 1 byte matters and optimize the word write to a byte write. The subsequent bytes in the word might not be zeroed.

This is highly theoretical - no known compiler does this. But for maximum security, the read-back should match the write size:
  (void)*(volatile unsigned long *)p;

Or read back all bytes individually.

Priority 3 - theoretical optimization escape.
close_reason: Fixed: The issue described a memcpy+byte-readback pattern that does not exist in the current code. The actual implementation uses volatile unsigned long* writes which preserve volatile semantics for the full word. Updated the misleading comment that referenced a union approach (not used) to accurately describe the cast-through-uintptr_t approach.
